title: 2025 11 12 - HE
displayTitle: 2025-11-12 - Health Committee - Briefing on the pharmacy sector
description: 12 November 2025 | Health Committee Briefing on the pharmacy sector Pharmaceutical Society of New Zealand Michael Hammond, Chairman of the Board & President Helen Morgan-Banda, Chief Executive Officer Petition of Meg Vardy: Review front-of-package nutritional labelling Meg Vardy Megan Tombs
descriptionSummary: Briefing on the pharmacy sector. Pharmaceutical Society of New Zealand. Michael Hammond, Chairman of the Board & President

chunks.text: Sector scheduled to hear from Pharmacy Sector Leaders Forum for 10 minutes. Let's, let's make it happen. Thank you. We will move to public session, please.
Thank you, Tracy. Okay. Thank you. Good afternoon. Afternoon everybody. Hello.

Welcome to the Health Committee. Very good to have you with us this afternoon. Uh, you're giving us a briefing on the pharmacy sector. We have you here for 10 minutes. Um, if you want to leave some time for time for questions, that'd be appreciated, but ultimately that's up to you and we have your document that you've sent around Fantastic to that too.

So we will hand that time over to You now. Great. Uh, so good afternoon everyone. I'm, um, Michael Han. I'm the current, uh, chair of the Pharmacy Sectors Leaders Forum, and also the chair and president of the Pharmaceutical Society of New Zealand. Uh, we'll most certainly leave some time for questions, um, at the end of during our 10 minute allotment. Absolutely. Um, so I also just wanna note sitting here, I've got the CEO of the Pharmaceutical Society of New Zealand, and our clinical director of and of policy, um, from PNZ, who also are the secretariat

for the PSLF um, group. So as, as you know, we're here to talk about the current and future state of pharmacy in New Zealand. Um, so kind of, you know, following along from the material we supplied you, who is PS Pharmacy Sectors Leaders Forum. It's made up of, uh, 13 organizations across the pharmacy sector. All the chairs or presidents or, um, and heads of school and also the pharmacy council form part of that membership.

So we canvas a whole way from primary hospital, general practice and academia and, um, the regulatory body. And so we are a group that is committed to a sustainable health system, and we are advocating for pharmacy, um, right across the system. And so, um, from, you know, the group agrees, you know, change is needed now.

Um, and with the right tools, the pharmacy forum can be a driver for that change. Uh, we, um, believe that the sector wants it, the pharmacy sector and the, and the public needs it from a, um, access point of view. So in terms of, uh, uh, some, you know, high level background on pharmacy, we're quite a young workforce, um, predominantly female.

Um, and our pharmacists, uh, um, you know, essentially everywhere medicines are, there are a thousand bricks and mortar pharmacies across the, across the country. So, you know, some fantastic access there as well as pharmacists integrated into general practice and into hospital. Um, unfortunately, um, we, in terms of our workforce sustainability, a lot of our young pharmacists are coming out.

Uh, they quickly become a bit, uh, disillusioned at, from their career, uh, from what they thought they were going to be doing and the pressures that they're facing and they're leaving moving overseas or into other, other careers. So, uh, you know, from that we've recognized that we really need some widespread recognition of all the things that, uh, pharmacy is doing beyond the role of dispensing, um, internationally.

Um, it's very clear that we've fallen behind what's happening in other countries. Um, there is, you know, significant untapped potential across the pharmacy sector. And it's very, from our feedback we get from our members, uh, and members of the PLF, is that pharmacists want to work at full scope. They want to do more.

Um, and the similar technicians, um, who work under the supervision of pharmacists, they to do more as well. And they believe they can do more for the health of New Zealanders. Uh, so it's quite, you know, it's quite clear, and I know you hear this from many other parts of the health system, that more, uh, more funding is needed or sustainable funding is needed for pharmacy.

And this is across all parts, community general practice, hospital, uh, with, you know, the right level of sustainable funding. They can do so much more in terms of access to medicines for, for patients. Um, and I think, you know, there is, we did a workforce survey, um, at PCNZ and that showed that our members were telling us, you know, that they're, uh, facing significant challenges, siping shortages, and that one of the key challenges for 'em is, is funding.

Um, so I've actually launched through my slides and content very quickly, um, which allows us quite significant time for questions. Excellent. Thank you very much. And, uh, members will have received those slides and through them, cam, you had your hand Up. Yeah, I just had a question to maybe kick it off and thank you very much for, um, bringing this to the committee.

We've got, uh, you've said, um, maybe just in a way to elucidate a conversation, uh, have you, what did, what did young pharmacists think they would be doing, which they are not doing? Uh, they thought they'd be fully utilizing the training that they received at university. And high level of pharmaceutical knowledge is, is, um, ingrained in these individuals.

So they really thought they'd be utilizing that to the fullness of their abilities to give high medicines optimization to patients high level, um, therapy. So it's linked to what you said about, um, working at the top of their scope. What is it that's stopping that happening? So one of the key areas where when we talk about full scope, that's relates to the realms of prescribing.

So you have pharmac right now, you've got pharmacists, prescribers, um, who would be fantastic as uh, uh, pharmacist practitioners. But then the next level down pharmacists have the skills to work as independent prescribers for more, like for some chronic condition management. Um, things that we used to talk about in the minor ailments scheme, which we know has, we can, but build on that.

Um, and so being able to use their skills in university in that way is what part part of that? Yeah. If anyone's got any sub. Alright. Well, I, I thought you you go, you No, no. Go. I've got a primary here. No, no, it's, I you've pretty much asked the same question. So guys going.

Yeah. Um, so talking about, um, working to the top, top of your scope, what are some of the conditions that you are seeing? Um, and I look, I suppose I'm mainly focusing on community pharmacies. Yeah. Because of also play a very important role in our primary healthcare. Um, and also in our community, increasing their access.

What are some of the conditions that, uh, pharmacists are being frustrated about that they can't actually in effect, uh, treat, um, when they're coming into the pharmacy? I'm gonna get, um, Chris to ask that because Chris receives these calls, um, to our, um, we call it pharmacist hotline, um, on a regular basis around, you know, what are my boundaries in this area?

It's got some fantastic examples. Thank You. Um, it's, uh, more around the, um, I suppose the things they can't do at the minute that they could do is around possibly chronic condition management. Um, a lot of what pharmacists do is around, as you know, you go into a pharmacy, you present your prescription, if there are issues around the prescription, they have to get those resolved. And that takes the time. Um, but they're focusing on the supply side of things rather than the clinical optimization of medicines.

So what they can't easily do at this moment in time, 'cause the way the system is designed is they can't actually, if you went in with a prescription and looked at it and go, okay, we could improve this therapy port, the general practitioner if could be, but a lot of it is locked in the processes they have to follow with regards to the supply. Sorry, can I just go just, just just further clarification.

What, what sort of things are you talking about? You're talking about dosage, you're talking about changing slightly, changing the medications. And do, do you have any examples of some of the conditions that that would be, would be affected? Uh, It could be dosage at the moment. Pharmacists do that with anticoagulation. So there's a service that's running across some parts of the country where warfarin is, um, titrated by the pharmacist on collaborative agreement with the general practitioner.

Um, allopurinol and the arthritis New Zealand will come and talk to you about increasing access to gout. So there's that sort of stuff that's coming online. So those, those sorts of services actually are given opportunity for pharmacists, utilize bits more of their skills. There's, um, example around, um, hba one C, um, see some people are doing tests in that space or blood pressure monitoring, and at the moment if something untoward is picked up, they referred back to their primary provider, which is appropriate at this moment in time.

But if there's an ability to work collaboratively in that space, so the patient doesn't necessarily have to go back to the general practitioner, if it's appropriate, then there's that sort of management that could occur. I could give you a really good real life example with, uh, Sarah who's here, who's also a pharmacist. Um, a few weeks ago I got shingles on my hand, which is unusual, but I did, and I had it before, so I knew what it was.

And I went and saw Sarah in Unichem and Cuba Street, uh, understanding orders, which know be great improvement from that, but understanding orders, she could do a consult with me. She looked at it, she took some, um, notes, my blood pressure, she was able to give me, um, medicine right there. And then my alternatives would've been to wait and get a GP visit on a Monday or go to after hours and pay $120 in Wellington.

And I think I paid, I think, was it $50? Yeah, $50. So done and dusted, had the drugs went home, that cleaned up shingles sooner. And shingles can't last for a long time. So it's actually really good to get it early. That's just an example of how the public can benefit. And that's a workaround at the moment. If we actually have that as access, um, it would be, you know, huge improvement.

Thank you. Come over to Tracy. Mm, thank You. Um, you mentioned the minor ailments scheme. So essentially what you're saying is, is um, sort of, um, building on that. So are there, and I'm presuming that you'll have access to some overseas analysis or modeling that have shown that kind of, that, that, um, system wide savings in terms of GP visits and other health visits.

Is there any key lines from that that you'd like to highlight? Key lines from your research that You've? Um, I would say that the example would be, um, either pharmacy first in, um, QB Farm in England, or pharmacy First in Scotland. They've shown some significant benefits and the government in Scotland have just, uh, commissioned some more work and some more resource into that space.

Mm-hmm. So it's, um, I can provide the evidence to you if you, if you would like it. I, I talk to Scotland quite frequently. Mm-hmm. Um, they're great to be collaborative internationally and learn from each other. So could that be great? I can share that, no problem. Yeah. And Anybody watching could Oh yeah. Anyone else? Anyone else watching? I can, I can provide it to the committee. I don't know how it works from that perspective, but yeah. Happy to provide.

Um, just to add, in Scotland, uh, 50% of the community pharmacists are prescribers. Mm-hmm. And they get a slight increase in their remuneration to do that. Mm-hmm. And if you go, I just recently went to London and most funds that have a big long list in the window of all the conditions that they can treat. Yeah. So, which we can't do some of them or we can't do others. And That's what you mean by the untapped?

Absolutely, yes. I stop on that, Jeff. Uh, hold on. I've go to Junior. Did you have Question? Just following up, uh, did you say 50% of, uh, pharmacies, was it in Scotland? In Scotland, yes. In Scotland. Do they have additional training? So the 50% that aren't mm-hmm. Do the prescribing pharmacists have any more training than the 50% that don't? Yes, they would Have would. Yeah.

They would. Yes. Yes. And I would, to be fair, I would expect training to be undertaken to ensure competence and safety for the public. I think that's, And and mindful this will be our last question. So my question is about your, um, 1000 community pharmacists and pharmacy. It is pharmacy, sorry. Um, and it is something that we need, especially among MP from South, but also rural.

Mm-hmm. Um, towns. My question is more about demand and supply. Do you have, um, some of your smaller community pharmacies actually tell you whether the, there are pricing differences, say between them and similar medications for a larger provider, say like chemist Warehouse, and do you have a projection, um, forward whether you would continue to have these community pharmacies, uh, into the future?

Or is there going to be a, a possible reduction because of competition from smaller pharmacies versus a bigger one? We certainly do receive feedback from our members on the challenges they face of having the different types of providers opening, especially in those rural, um, the rural environments. It does create a challenge for them.

Um, we haven't done any projections in terms of how many we expect might close over the coming years. We hope they don't close, but we do. Um, we haven't doing some work to help try and strengthen our rural workforce and our rural pharmacies because that is a, um, even bigger challenge than the big centers. So you don't know whether there are farm community pharmacies that have closed over the last, I don't know, five, 10 years or So, we, we can provide you with, um, there's a really good piece of research that's been done recently.

And they've talked about pharmacy, excuse me, pharmacy deserts in New Zealand, and that's about rural New Zealand where you just, pharmacists are closed and you, they just can't have someone. And one of our board members is from mfi. It's getting people to come, it's getting people to train, it's getting people to stay. Right. So it's just that, so you're, you're right, these in Terms terms of funding, 'cause you asked about more funding, I just wondered whether that was also part of The picture. And, and rural is a real challenge for some

of our pharmacies for sustainability. Um, irrespective of who's coming into the market, um, we're aware of, I'm aware of one pharmacy that I worked with, um, during the COVID pandemic. He was really struggling to get access because of his location. I think he was 260 kilometers from the nearest other pharmacy. Um, but unfortunately, and he was providing services to the other side of the Gulf where he was based.

Um, unfortunately he was unable to sustain his business and actually has had to close. So the nearest pharmacy for that group of people is about 260 K, which is a long way for people to access healthcare. Thank you. Thank you for making yourself available. Thank, thank you very much. Appreciate your time. Thank you. Thank you
Session.

Meg, very good to have you here. Welcome to the health committee. I'm Sam Dale. I have a number of members here as well. We have you here for 10 minutes. And hello Megan as well. Good to have you along. Hi there. We have you here for 10 minutes. You can use all that time to speak if you wish, or you may leave us time for questions. Always nice to have some time for questions, but, um, ultimately that's over to you.

So I will hand that time to you now. Thank you. Fantastic. Okay, so I would like to, um, leave us open for questions, but I'd just quickly like to review what it is that we are here for today. Um, thank you so much for making the time for us to come along and speak with you. Um, so we are here to represent the over 400,000 people who will be affected by an eating disorder in their lifetime in AA to voice concern over the prospect of either mandatory health star ratings on all foods or, um, alternatives such as warning labels being implemented.

So we're requesting that the House of Representatives incorporates eating disorder lived experience into the review of Health Star ratings to protect those with or at greater risk of developing an eating disorder. Um, this is in line with the new eating disorder strategy that was re uh, that was launched earlier this year that demands that eating disorders are considered across sectors.

Um, uh, alongside that, we recommend that Health Star ratings are discontinued, um, and that warning labels are not implemented. That government explores alternative approaches to incent incentivizing product reformulation such as Perian tax. Um, and again, that, um, eating disorder voices are incorporated in this process.

Um, so just very briefly, there are two broad arguments for Health star ratings. The first is that they change consumer practices, the second is reformulation. So, um, in, in relation to that first point, um, there's no evidence that health Healthstar ratings actually change consumer choices.

A 2022 study, Inoa found little or no relationship between healthstar ratings and products purchased. Um, 44 countries have implemented some form of front of, um, package nutritional label, and there's little to no evidence that this has had any, um, effect on population health. In terms of the second point, um, it is true that health star ratings have contributed to reformulation approximately 79% of products displaying health star ratings have been reformulated.

However, um, this process can happen without consumer awareness and avoiding any impact on consumer. Um, and that's where an option like Pion tax comes in. So by that we're talking about sugar tax. So an example in the uk, the implementation of sugar tax has led to, um, uh, vast reformulation, and that has, um, translated into changes in population health.

Um, but what we really wanna highlight is that there's been no research, um, on, um, the impacts of, um, health star ratings on people living with eating disorders and very little on front of packaged nutritional labels and the impact of that on people with eating disorders. So we conducted a survey of 110 people, um, uh, 101 of those had lived experience of an eating disorder.

Um, 80% of our respondents believe that health star ratings and warning labels are harmful for people with eating disorders. Um, people reported that it restricted their ability to make choices around food, that it reinforced eating disorder beliefs around good and bad food, that it instilled a, a sense of guilt and shame around food, um, that may be regarded as healthy, but is nevertheless critical for, um, recovery.

Um, and some people reported that they only bought five star products, um, and that it, that it, um, fed into orthorexic tendencies. Me as someone with lived experience of anorexia nervosa, um, uh, I still to this day struggle to purchase products that have a health star rating on them, because to me, that's a moralization of food that I don't need in my life when I am, um, every day, uh, fighting against the eating disorder that, that, that I have, I have well already fought so hard to, to live my life in spite of.

Um, so just to conclude, and I wanna, um, then make space for questions. Um, the approach to using front of package nutritional labels has been compared to warning labels on cigarettes. Um, but food is not the same as cigarettes. No one is advocating that people stop eating. Um, but that's what can happen. Um, with healthstar ratings, um, uh, poorly mentored, poorly implemented front of package nutritional labels, promote the moralization and demonization of food, impose a risk of undue harm to people with or at risk of developing an eating disorder.

Um, this is a voice that remains unheard in policy discussions. Um, so that's, that's my spiel. Um, I would love to make space now for questions. Um, Mr. Chair. Thank you, Meg. Thank you very much. Meg? Yes. Hey, me, you have a question? Sorry. Thank, thank you, Meg.

So the thing is just, just a point, point of clarification. On one hand, you are saying that the health star ratings doesn't change consumer behavior. Mm-hmm. But on the other hand, are, are you saying that people with eating disorders, uh, make up that small percentage of people that do change their consumer behavior because of that Health Star rating?

So that's a, it's, that's a really good question. So I guess what I'm saying is that there's no evidence, I mean, no research has been done on the impact, um, uh, of health star ratings on people with eating disorders. When we look at research conducted on the impact of health star ratings on the general population and consumer habits, um, then there's no evidence that they lead to the positive changes that people want to see.

Um, uh, so that would be changes in, in, um, consumer habits that might align with, with, um, population understanding of, of healthy food. Um, there isn't any research yet on, um, the impact of people living with eating disorders. Um, apart from the small survey that we've done.

And what we're asking for is that you take, is that, is that, is that you take that into account, um, when, um, looking at the review of Health star ratings and what we implement going forward, does that clarify? Yeah, kind of. Sorry. Um, but you're, say you're saying that it's really bad, but then it doesn't have much effect in consumer behavior.

I suppose that's, that's the kind of thing that I'm, that I'm just grappling with at the moment. I could see Megan putting her hand up, so I might, I was thinking, hello. Thank you for, um, talking with us. I think to clarify that perhaps it's good to look at some numbers so that, um, the latest eating disorder, uh, strategy that was launched by our current government, um, identified that up to 7.8% of New Zealanders will experience an eating disorder in their lifetime.

That translates to over 400,000 people. Um, so although we are saying the general population, it doesn't change their behavior. We know that people with an eating disorder, um, can be affected by these, um, gold stars if you like the stars on front, front, front of packaging.

Yeah. Um, so perhaps I think what Meg is saying is we are asking for a voice at the table. Um, that's What I, Because those data don't exist yet. Um, for people living with eating disorders and absence of evidence is not evidence of absence. Mm. Cam no, they should just answer my question. Okay.

Thank you. Do any other members have questions? I would, Dr. Ra and love and thank you Meg, um, in particular, and Megan as well for, for your support. But Meg, thank you very much for your submission. It's always really good for the committee to hear from people who lived experience on these issues.
Um, appreciate the courage it takes for you to come forward, uh, present, live as you have done this afternoon. Kiara, Thank you. Thank you very much. Enjoy your afternoon.
